name: Amphisbaena
primary_sources: Naturalis Historia by Pliny the Elder
The Book of Beasts by T.H. White

visual_attributes.morphology: serpent with a head at each end
distinctive_features: twin heads, one at each end
glowing eyes like candles or lightning
visual_attributes.structural_arrangement: heads at both ends, capable of moving in either direction

domain_specific_visual.mythological_variations: sometimes depicted with chicken feet, feathered wings, or as a horned dragon-like creature

secondary_functions: used in folk medicine and magical remedies

domain_specific_functional.literary_symbolism: represents the animal by which the Fall was caused in Paradise Lost

relationships.description: Spawned from the blood of Medusa's head
Encountered by Cato's army in Libya

contextual_attributes.geographic_range: Libyan Desert, referenced in Greek and Roman texts
contextual_attributes.cultural_significance: Symbol of duality, used in folk medicine
primary_sources: Naturalis Historia by Pliny the Elder

contrastive_constraints: NOT a purely fictional creature without mythological context
